coredata.dc:title: CuNCs assisted by carbon dots to construct Z-type heterostructures: Ultra-high photocatalytic performance and peroxidase-like activity
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Dan
Lei, Xiangshan
Liu, Qingdong
Chen, Yajun
Wang, Jiao
Han, Bingyan
He, Gaohong
coredata.dc:description: Copper nanoclusters (CuNCs), as novel energy level tunable photocatalysts, have been utilized to combine with various conventional inorganic catalysts to improve catalytic performance. However, conventional photocatalysts generally have fixed energy levels, which are not conducive to bi-directional adjustment of the composite catalyst energy level structure. In this work, CuNCs protected by 3-mercaptopropionic acid (CuNCs@3-MPA) and energy-level controllable carbon dots (CDs) are combined to prepare intelligent photocatalytic materials through electrostatic self-assembly strategy, i.e., CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs. The self-assembled CuNCs@3-MPA have a large specific surface area, and the Z-type heterostructure can be constructed by introducing energy-matched CDs, which can achieve the effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and directional generation of ·O2 − at the same time, enabling the degradation of 98.5 % Methyl Orange (MO, 20 mg/L) in 7 min. The catalytic rate constant is increased to 0.501 min−1, which is equivalent to 3.6 times that of CuNCs@3-MPA alone. Since CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs with rich Cu(I) can perform Fenton-like reaction to catalyze the production of ·OH from H2O2, we use TMB and H2O2 as substrates to probe its peroxidase-like activity. The Km is 0.18 mM when the substrate is TMB, which is lower than that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) (Km = 0.434 mM), indicating that the affinity between CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs and TMB is higher than that of HRP, which has a high peroxidase-like activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Carbon dots
Z-type heterostructures
Photocatalytic
Peroxidase-like enzyme

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Liaoning Key Laboratory of Chemical Additive Synthesis and Separation (ZJKF2103 & ZJKF2011) and Science Fund for Creative Research Groups of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22021005).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A–B) SEM and HR-TEM images of CuNCs@3-MPA, (C–H) EDX spectrum and EDX mapping images of CuNCs@3-MPA, (I-J) SEM and HR-TEM images of CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs, (K-Q) EDX spectrum and EDX mapping images of CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs (Insides are images of CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs in visible light.), (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs (Inside are the images of CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs under 365 nm UV excitation.), (C) XPS whole spectrum of Cu NCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs, (D) High-resolution XPS N 1s spectra of CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs, (E) High-resolution XPS S 2p spectra of Cu NCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs, (F) AES spectra of Cu elements in Cu NCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs.

(A) Catalytic effect of CuNCs@3-MPA with different CDs loading on MO, (B) dosage probes of CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs catalyzing RhB and MO, respectively, (C) pH probes of CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs catalyzing RhB and MO, respectively. Catalytic degradation of MO by CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs: (D) Ct/C0 graphs with time, (E) MO absorbance spectra with time, (F) Kinetic simulations.

(A) The degradation effect of CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs for MO in the presence of each scavenger, (B) Energy level structure and catalytic mechanism diagram of CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs, (C) Cycle diagram of CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs degradation of MO.

(A) CDs loading, (B) pH effect on CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs-like peroxidase, (C) Absorbance comparison of the system reaction for 10 min with and without the addition of CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs, (D) TAOH emission spectra of CDs, CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs in the presence and absence of H2O2, (E) Steady-state kinetic analysis of CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs with TMB as substrate according to Michaelis-Menten formula, (F) Corresponding double inverse plot.
Schematic diagram of synthesis of CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs.
Catalytic effect of CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs and other photocatalysts on MO.

head.ce:title: CuNCs assisted by carbon dots to construct Z-type heterostructures: Ultra-high photocatalytic performance and peroxidase-like activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Panjin 124221, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Dalian University of Technology, Panjin 124221, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Key Laboratory of Chemical Additive Synthesis and Separation, Panjin Institute of Industrial Technology, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Key Laboratory of Chemical Additive Synthesis and Separation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liaoning Key Laboratory of Chemical Additive Synthesis and Separation, Panjin Institute of Industrial Technology, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CuNCs assisted by carbon dots to construct Z-type heterostructures: ultra-high photocatalytic performance and peroxidase-like activity.

Copper nanoclusters (CuNCs), as novel energy level tunable photocatalysts, have been utilized to combine with various conventional inorganic catalysts to improve catalytic performance. However, conventional photocatalysts generally have fixed energy levels, which are not conducive to bi-directional adjustment of the composite catalyst energy level structure. In this work, CuNCs protected by 3-mercaptopropionic acid (CuNCs@3-MPA) and energy-level controllable carbon dots (CDs) are combined to prepare intelligent photocatalytic materials through electrostatic self-assembly strategy, i.e., CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs. The self-assembled CuNCs@3-MPA have a large specific surface area, and the Z-type heterostructure can be constructed by introducing energy-matched CDs, which can achieve the effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and directional generation of ·O2 − at the same time, enabling the degradation of 98.5 % Methyl Orange (MO, 20 mg/L) in 7 min. The catalytic rate constant is increased to 0.501 min−1, which is equivalent to 3.6 times that of CuNCs@3-MPA alone. Since CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs with rich Cu(I) can perform Fenton-like reaction to catalyze the production of ·OH from H2O2, we use TMB and H2O2 as substrates to probe its peroxidase-like activity. The Km is 0.18 mM when the substrate is TMB, which is lower than that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) (Km = 0.434 mM), indicating that the affinity between CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs and TMB is higher than that of HRP, which has a high peroxidase-like activity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The discharge of industrial, agricultural and domestic wastewater has a serious contamination on land and rivers, and the search for green and efficient wastewater treatment methods is urgent [1–4]. Photocatalytic oxidation utilizes renewable energy sources such as solar energy to completely degrade organic pollutants, which is in line with the concept of sustainable development and has attracted much attention [5,6]. Traditional photocatalysts such as TiO2, ZnO and CdS have problems such as band gap width and photocorrosion that seriously hinder the improvement of catalytic performance, and it is imperative to develop new photocatalysts [7–9]. Copper nanoclusters (CuNCs), as a new type of luminescent material, the nanoscale size gives it a dispersed energy level structure [10], which holds good prospects for its use in catalysis. The current research focuses on grafting CuNCs onto inorganic materials such as TiO2, g-C3N4, niobate, etc [11–13]. to improve the photocatalytic performance. For example, Ji et al. constructed Cu(I) clusters on the TiO2 surface by an in situ assembly technique, and used the sensitization effect of Cu(I) clusters to broaden the visible light absorption range and achieve effective charge transfer to obtain efficient Cr (VI) reduction [14]. Zhang et al. anchored CuNCs to g-C3N4 by thermal polymerization to promote the formation of charge transfer channels and enhance photocatalytic activity [15]. Most of these works use monodisperse CuNCs as a bridge for electron transfer and do not reflect the initiative role of CuNCs with tunable energy levels. If CuNCs are combined with intelligent materials for energy level matching and constructing heterojunctions, it will be promising to selectively synthesize photocatalytic materials with precise energy level structures and directed to generate the desired radicals to control the catalytic reactions based on the realization of effective charge transfer.

Carbon dots (CDs) are 0-dimensional nanomaterials, generally smaller than 10 nm in size, simple to prepare and inexpensive, have been widely studied since they were first discovered by Xu et al. in 2004 [16]. The modulation of the energy level of CDs has been achieved by quantum size effect, surface functional groups, atomic doping, etc [17–19]. Combining CDs and CuNCs, on one hand, the energy level difference between CuNCs and CDs can be used to construct specific heterojunctions [20], promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and improve the catalytic performance. On the other hand, the energy level structure can be bi-directionally adjusted to match different radical potentials for controlled generation of radicals. To address the problem that both CDs and CuNCs are 0-dimensional nanomaterials, which are not beneficial for their binding and catalyst recycling, a self-assembly of CuNCs is attempted to provide attachment sites for CDs and increase the catalyst size for recycling.

Herein, we successfully prepare CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs multifunctional binary nanosheets by a simple electrostatic self-assembly strategy, as shown in Scheme 1 . The self-assembled sheet structure of CuNCs@3-MPA has a large specific surface area, which is favorable for the adsorption of substrates and the attachment of CDs, and the increase of catalyst particle size also reduces the difficulty of recovery. Meanwhile, a Z-type heterogeneous structure is formed by the energy level difference configuration of CDs and CuNCs@3-MPA, so that the electrons on the LUMO orbitals of CuNCs@3-MPA are transferred to the HOMO orbitals of CDs, and the efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pair separation is improved. The introduction of CDs also shifts the overall energy level of the catalyst in the direction favorable to the generation of ·O2 −, which greatly enhances the catalyst performance. The results show that CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs achieve 98.5 % degradation of 20 mg/L Methyl Orange (MO) in 7 min. Also, CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs exhibits excellent peroxidase-like activity due to its abundant Cu(I) content for Fenton-like reaction, thus exhibiting catalytic effect on the substrate TMB with a Km value of 0.18 mM, which is much lower than that of natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP, Km = 0.434 mM). In summary, CuNCs@3-MPA assisted by CDs to construct Z-type heterojunctions possess ultra-high catalytic effect and excellent peroxidase-like activity.

All chemicals used were purchased from commercial sources and used without further purification. Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Rhodamine B (RhB), L-Phenylalanine, 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and 1,4-dicarboxybenzene (TA) were purchased from Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). 3-Mercaptopropionic acid (3-MPA), O-Phenylenediamine and Nitrotetrazolium Blue chloride (NBT) were purchased from Aladdin Chemicals Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Benzoquinone (BQ) and isopropanol (IPA) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Methyl Orange (MO) and triethanolamine (TEA) was purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 %) was purchased from Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Ultrapure water was used for all the experiments.

To prepare 10 mM, 50 mL of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O solution and 0.1 M, 100 mL of 3-MPA solution, respectively. Slowly added the Cu(NO3)2 solution to the 3-MPA solution and kept stirring. The solution turned milky white. Kept stirring for 4 h and left to stand for 1 h. The white precipitate was self-assembled CuNCs@3-MPA.

The CDs were prepared using our previous work [21]. Dissolved 0.1 g of o-phenylenedimethylamine and 0.1 g of phenylalanine in 7 mL of water and 5 mL of sulfuric acid. Then sonicated for 30 min. Transferred to Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclaves and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove insoluble large particles. Subsequently, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Finally, the CDs powder was prepared by dialysis using a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 24 h to remove salt and acid.

0.00025 g, 0.0005 g, 0.001 g, 0.002 g, 0.004 g of CDs powder were weighed into 10 mM, 50 mL of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O solution, stirred well, and then added into 5 portions of 0.1 M, 100 mL of 3-MPA solution and stirred for 4 h. The light purple precipitates obtained after standing were the CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs with different CDs loadings.

Zeta potential was tested using Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZSE. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was performed on FEI Nova Nano SEM 450. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was made on FEI Tecnai G2 F20. The UV–visible absorption spectra were obtained by a Hitachi UH5300 double-beam UV–visible spectrophotometer from Japan. The fluorescence spectra (PL) were recorded with a Hitachi F7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Ultraviolet Photoelectron Spectroscopy (UPS) were carried out on Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi spectrometer. The mass spectra is analyzed by a quadrupole orbiting ion trap high-resolution mass spectrometer of the Q-Exactive model from Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA.

MO and RhB were used to investigate the photocatalytic activities of CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs. 5 mg of freeze-dried CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs were added to 20 mg/L, 8 mL of MO solution, respectively, and dark adsorbed for 2 min before moving them to the 300 W xenon lamp for degradation. The absorbance was monitored every 1 min. 20 mg of freeze-dried CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs were added to 10 mg/L, 8 mL of RhB solution, respectively, and dark adsorbed for 6 min before moving them to a 300 W xenon lamp for degradation. The absorbance was monitored every 3 min. BQ, IPA and TEA were used as scavengers of ·O2 −, ·OH and h+, respectively, added during the catalytic MO to investigate the components that play a major role in the catalytic process.

Under the optimal test conditions, 30 μL of H2O2 (30 % aqueous solution) was added to 3 mL of 0.5 mM TMB solution and the absorbance of the solution was measured for 10 min in a dark room to monitor the effect of H2O2 on the oxidation of TMB without the addition of catalyst. Meanwhile, 30 μL of H2O2 (30 % aqueous solution) was added to 3 mL, 0.5 mM of TMB solution and 1 mg of CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs was immediately added to this mixed solution as peroxidase-like enzyme, and the effect of CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs on the oxidation of TMB was monitored by measuring the absorbance of the solution after standing for 10 min in a dark room.

The kinetics of the reaction was studied on the basis of varying the TMB and H2O2 concentration [22]. In calculating the TMB-ox concentration, it was necessary to convert the absorbance to concentration according to Lambert beer's law,
A = ε × b × C
A is the absorbance measured by the instrument, ε is the molar extinction coefficient of TMB-ox of 39000 M−1cm−1, and b is the path length of light which is 1 cm.

Fitting with the Michaelis-Menten equation,
1 υ
=
K m
V
max
1
[ S ]
+
1
V
max
Vmax is the maximum reaction velocity; Km is the Michaelis constant; [S] is the concentration of the reaction substrate. The smaller the Km , the higher the affinity of the catalyst to the substrate.

CuNCs@3-MPA is prepared using 3-Mercaptopropionic acid (3-MPA) as ligand and reducing agent, and the SEM image shows nanosheets, as shown in Fig. 1 (A). And the high-resolution TEM in Fig. 1(B) shows that they contain a large number of pores, which are favorable for substrate adsorption. Observing Fig. 1(C–H), it can be seen that Cu, C, O, S in EDS mapping originates from the metal cores and surface ligands of CuNCs@3-MPA. Zeta potential results, shown in Fig. S1, indicate that as-prepared CuNCs@3-MPA with −10.3 mv negative potential can form heterogeneous structures with CDs with 10.14 mv positive potential through electrostatic interaction. After loading CDs, the nanosheet structure of CuNCs@3-MPA is not disrupted (Fig. 1(I)), and from the high-resolution TEM in Fig. 1(J), the CDs are uniformly located on the surface of the self-assembled CuNCs@3-MPA. N elements from the CDs are found in the EDX mapping, which also demonstrates the successful deposition of CDs on the surface of CuNCs@3-MPA (Fig. 1(K–Q)).

UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CuNCs@3-MPA, shown in Fig. 2 (A), exhibits a characteristic absorption peak at 350 nm, compared to which CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs show a new UV absorption peak at 262 nm, which is attributed to π-π* transitions of CC [21], indicating the effective deposition of CDs on CuNCs@3-MPA. The fluorescence spectra and images under 365 nm UV excitation (Fig. 2(B)) show that as-prepared CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs are almost non-luminescent, guessing the low recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which is favorable to improve the photocatalytic performance [23]. XPS is used to analyze the stability of CuNCs@3-MPA and the oxidation state of Cu elements before and after the loading of CDs. Due to low loading of the CDs, the full XPS spectrum as in Fig. 2 (C–D) shows only a faint N peak at 400.56 eV. Two peaks at 162.69 eV and 163.88 eV are obtained after deconvolution of the high-resolution S 2p spectrum (Fig. 2(E)), which are attributed to the formation of Cu-S bonds between CuNCs@3-MPA and S-C bonds in the ligand itself, respectively [24]. The characteristic peaks of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 appear at 932.1 eV and 952.1 eV, and the absence of peaks at 942 eV suggests that Cu(II) is not present in the system. However, since the XPS peaks of Cu(0) and Cu(I) differ by only 0.1 eV [25], the Auger electron spectra (AES) are used to better distinguish them, and the emergence of peaks at 916.4 eV and 918.9 eV shows the existence of a large amount of Cu(I) and Cu(0) in the system, as can be seen in Fig. 2(F). Except for the N element, the XPS peaks of each element of CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs are almost unchanged (Fig. S3, Tables S1, S2), once again demonstrating that the structure of CuNCs@3-MPA remains stable after loading CDs.

The photocatalytic performance of CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs is evaluated with Methyl Orange (MO) and Rhodamine B (RhB). Firstly, different loading of CDs is investigated on the catalytic performance, and it is observed from Fig. 3 (A) that CuNCs@3-MPA alone can only degrade 62.6 % of MO in 7 min, and the degradation rate increases to 98.5 % with the addition of CDs up to 0.001 g, but the catalytic efficiency decreases to 60 % with more adding of CDs. We speculate that loading the proper amount of CDs facilitates the separation of photogenerated electrons-hole and provides abundant active sites for the reaction, while the energy level can be tuned to promote the photocatalytic reaction. In contrast, excess CDs can lead to self-agglomeration and reduce the photocatalytic performance, as shown in Fig. S4, which shows the HR-TEM of CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs with the loading of CDs at 0.00025 g, 0.001 g and 0.004 g, respectively. It can be observed that only a few scattered CDs are observed on CuNCs@3-MPA at a loading of 0.00025 g. When the loading amount increases to 0.001 g, the CDs are distributed more uniformly on CuNCs@3-MPA. However, when the loading is increased to 0.004 g, a severe aggregation behavior occurrs between CDs and CDs, which is not conducive to the uniform distribution of CDs on the surface of CuNCs@3-MPA and the display of active sites, resulting in a lower photocatalytic efficiency. It is noteworthy that for RhB, although the catalytic efficiency also changed similarly to that of catalytic MO after loading CDs, CuNCs@3-MPA shows the highest catalytic effect with a degradation rate of 98.9 % in 15 min (Fig. S5). Considering that CuNCs@3-MPA are more negative than CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs, so higher adsorption can be achieved by electrostatic attraction for cationic dye RhB between them, exhibiting higher catalytic effect relative to CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs. For anionic dye MO, electrostatic attraction between them is much lower than that of RhB, but CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs shows 98.5 % catalytic degradation effect. This is likely from more efficient catalytic process. As shown in the Fig. 3(B), 2.5 g/L and 0.625 g/L of CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs are regarded as the optimal experimental amounts. Meanwhile, the catalytic effect can be affected dramatically by pH with the range of 1–11, shown in Fig. 3(C). The highest degradation rates of RhB and MO are observed at pH = 3, which is consistent with that of the synthesis environment of CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs. Upon high pH, the self-assembled structure of CuNCs@3-MPA disintegrates, leading to a decrease in the catalytic rate. At low pH, we speculate that the reduction in catalytic rate may be attributed to a decrease in photocatalytic radical production.

Kinetic profiles of CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs under optimal conditions are explored. As expected, CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs show excellent adsorption effects on RhB, especially CuNCs@3-MPA, which shows a more negative potential on the surface charge, and can adsorb 22.5 % of RhB within 6 min. Comparatively, owing to the better adsorption effect of CuNCs@3-MPA, 98.9 % of RhB is degraded within 15 min, while CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs, which has a slightly lower degradation rate of 86.0 %, shown in Fig. S6(A, B). The first order kinetic curves for the degradation of RhB by CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs are provided in Fig. S6(C), and CuNCs@3-MPA exhibits a higher rate constant (k) of 0.265 min−1, showing good catalytic performance compared to other materials, as shown in Table S3. However, the higher adsorption effect of CuNCs@3-MPA also makes it more susceptible to be poisoned. Fig. S7 shows that the degradation rate of CuNCs@3-MPA decreases to 34.8 % after recycling for 5 times, and when comparing with the morphology before catalysis, CuNCs@3-MPA obviously changes to the pink color of RhB (Fig. S8). This indicates that a large amount of RhB is adsorbed onto CuNCs@3-MPA and cannot be desorbed, so that the reaction site is covered by RhB, which significantly reduce catalytic activity. In order to exclude the influence of adsorption on the experiment and better explore the catalytic mechanism, we chose the anionic dye MO as a research model. Fig. 3(D) demonstrates that only 1–2 % of MO is adsorbed during the dark adsorption process, while CuNCs@3-MPA shows a better catalytic effect with the help of CDs, degrading 98.5 % of MO within 7 min. Fig. 3(E) and (F) show the variation curve of MO absorbance with catalytic time, and the degradation process is also in accordance with the first order kinetics with a k value of 0.501 min−1, which is 3.6 times higher than that of CuNCs@3-MPA. Compared with conventional catalysts such as TiO2, ZnO and other Cu-containing catalysts, CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs have much higher rate constants, indicating that it possesses ultra-high catalytic performance, as summarized in Table 1
[26–32].

The photocatalytic mechanism is investigated using benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA), and triethanolamine (TEA) as capture agents for ·O2 −, ·OH, and h+, respectively. As shown in the Fig. 4 (A) and Table S4, the degradation of MO by CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs reaches 98.5 %, without the addition of any capture agent. However, the degradation rate drops dramatically to 59.94 % with the adding of BQ, while IPA and TEA have a weak effect on the degradation rate, indicating that ·O2 − plays a decisive role in the catalytic degradation of MO. Fig. S9(A, B) illustrates that the energy level gaps of CuNCs@3-MPA and CDs are is calculated to be 2.69 eV and 1.96 eV by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, respectively. The EVB of CuNCs@3-MPA and CDs are also measured to be 0.14 eV and −0.65 eV (Fig. S9(C–F)), by UPS (UV photoelectron spectroscopy), respectively. Therefore, their ECB are calculated as 2.83 eV and 1.31 eV, respectively, based on the equation (1):
(1)
E
VB
=
E
CB
+
E g

As can be seen in Fig. 4(B), the ECB of CuNCs@3-MPA has a higher potential (0.14 eV vs. NHE) relative to O2/·O2 − (−0.33 eV vs. NHE) and do not generate ·O2 −, while the EVB has a higher potential (2.83 eV vs. NHE) than ·OH/H2O (+2.38 eV vs. NHE) for the generation of ·OH. As shown in Fig. S10, since terephthalic acid (TA) combines with ·OH can emit fluorescence from the product 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid (TAOH) and the fluorescence intensity is proportional to the amount of ·OH, it is used to detect the ·OH generated during the photocatalysis process [22,33]. Fig. S11(A) shows the fluorescence spectra of TAOH in the presence of CuNCs@3-MPA for 0 min, 10 min and 20 min under light, displaying a gradual increase in the fluorescence characteristic peak of TAOH. In contrast, CuNCs@3-MPA placed under a dark room for 20 min has no TAOH characteristic peak, as shown in Fig. S11(B), indicating that the photocatalytic process of CuNCs@3-MPA produced ·OH. In view of the degradation effect of CuNCs@3-MPA on MO, we speculate that ·OH also plays a role in catalytic MO, but its role seems insignificant compared to ·O2 −. Therefore, in the radical trapping experiments, even the addition of ·OH trapping agent does not have much effect on the catalytic effect due to the presence of ·O2 −. When CDs are deposited on CuNCs@3-MPA to form a Z-type heterojunction, the electrons on the ECB of CuNCs@3-MPA migrate to the holes of the EVB of CDs due to the presence of energy level difference, resulting in an effective separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Considering that the ECB potential of CDs is lower than that of O2/·O2 −, the complex can produce both ·O2 − and ·OH. Nitrotetrazolium Blue chloride (NBT) is reduced to a water-insoluble blue formazan after binding ·O2 −, as shown in Fig. S12, and shows a characteristic peak at 560 nm, which is often used to detect ·O2 − [22,34]. Herein, NBT and TA are used to detect ·O2 − and ·OH produced by CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs composite photocatalyst. After adding NBT to CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs aqueous solution and photocatalyzing for 20 min, a blue-purple precipitate is produced, and a characteristic UV peak at 560 nm is observed when it is dissolved in ethanol. However, this phenomenon is absent after 0 min of photocatalysis and 20 min left in the dark, as shown in Fig. S13(A, B), indicating that ·O2 − is produced during the photocatalysis of CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs. The ·OH generated during the catalytic process is detected using TA, and the characteristic peak of TAOH is continuously enhanced after 10 min and 20 min of light, and this phenomenon is not observed under dark conditions (Fig. S14(A, B)), indicating that CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs also produce ·OH during the photocatalytic process. In addition, we find that the characteristic peak of TAOH in the presence of CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs is more obvious than single CuNCs@3-MPA. This is attributed to Z-type heterojunctions between CDs and CuNCs@3-MPA, which accelerates the electron-hole pair separation, and then generates more ·OH. ·O2 − acts as the rate-limiting step for the catalytic degradation of MO, which makes CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs exhibit a more excellent catalytic effect. In conclusion, the energy level structure and catalytic mechanism of CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs are shown in Fig. 4(B). MO binds to ·OH and ·O2 − generated by CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs and is degraded to small molecules. The degradation products are analyzed by ESI-MS and the processes are speculated as shown in Fig. S15 and S16. Fig. 4(C) reveals that CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs for degrading MO can still maintain more than 90 % after 10 cycles. However, when the catalyst is cycled to the 20th time, only 20 % of MO is degraded within 7 min. If the catalytic time is extend to 90 min, MO can still be completely degraded and retain its original appearance shown in Fig. S17 and S18, indicating that the catalyst still works, and that the decreased catalytic efficiency may be due to partial loss of catalyst during recycling. Meanwhile, we detect the generation of photocatalytic radicals at pH = 1 and 3 to investigate the reason for the decrease in the photocatalytic rate at pH = 1. As shown in Figs. S19–S21, the characteristic peaks of indicators for ·OH and ·O2 − in the presence of CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs decrease at pH = 1 compared to pH = 3, respectively. It indicates that the amount of generated photocatalytic radicals decrease when the pH is too low, which leads to the decline of photocatalytic efficiency, and this is consistent with the previous conjecture. On the other hand, ·OH is produced by the combination of H2O with h+, as shown in the following equation:
H 2
O +
h
+
→
H
+
+ ∙ O H

The production of ·OH is accompanied by the production of H+, so we speculate that the excess H+ at pH = 1 is not favorable for the reaction to proceed positively to produce ·OH, and therefore the photocatalytic efficiency is reduced relative to the pH = 3.

In addition, AES spectra show a large amount of Cu(I) in the as-obtained CuNCs@3-MPA and CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs, and then we estimate that they will have peroxidase-like effects by Fenton-like reactions in the absence of light. The enzymatic activity is explored using TMB and H2O2 as the catalytic substrates, and the reaction is carried out at different CDs-loadings and pH. The highest enzyme catalytic rate is achieved at CDs-loading of 0.001 g and pH = 4.6, as shown in Fig. 5 (A, B). In the presence of CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs, shown in Fig. 5C, TMB solution quickly turn blue within 10 min, showing a higher absorption peak at 652 nm. In contrast, for the H2O2 system alone, the absorbance change at 652 nm is not significant, further indicating the peroxidase-like effect of CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs. Fig. S22 shows that both CuNCs@3-MPA and CDs can catalyze TMB and show the characteristic peak at 652 nm. The ·OH generated during the catalytic process is detected using TA, as shown in Fig. 5(D), the TAOH peak is strongest catalyzed by CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs in the presence of H2O2. When H2O2 is absent, there is no TAOH characteristic peak, therefore without ·OH production, so we conclude that this higher enzymatic catalytic activity is attributed to the synergistic effect of CuNCs@3-MPA and CDs to produce more ·OH from H2O2, and subsequently TMB binds to ·OH to form TMB-ox. The kinetics of enzyme-catalyzed reaction of CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs-like peroxidase is studied by controlling the amount of substrates TMB and H2O2. Where the Michaelis-Menten equation reacts the starting speed of enzymatic reaction with the substrate concentration:
(2)
υ 0
=
V
max
S
K m
+
S

Km is the Michaelis constant, which can reflect the affinity of enzyme and substrate, the smaller the Km , the higher the affinity of enzyme and substrate, and Vmax is the maximum reaction speed [35]. At low substrate concentration, it is first order reaction, for which we plot the relationship between substrate and initial reaction rate as shown in Fig. 5(E) and Fig. S23(A). To reduce the difficulty of calculation, the double-reciprocal curve is drawn by taking the inverse of the horizontal and vertical coordinates, as shown in Fig. 5(F) and S23(B), and the Km and Vmax values are calculated according to the conversion formula of the Michaelis-Menten equation:
(3)
1 υ
=
K m
V
max
1
S
+
1
V
max

When the substrate is TMB, the Km and Vmax values are 0.18 mM and 18.8 × 10−8 M/s, the lower Km compared to natural horseradish peroxidase and most artificial enzymes indicates that CuNCs@3-MPA/0.001CDs has a higher affinity with TMB, and the higher Vmax value also suggests its good catalytic performance. When the substrate is H2O2, the Vmax reach to 104 × 10−8 M/s, which increase significantly. Although Km is 171.9 mM, Vmax is higher relative to HRP and other artificial enzymes, as shown in Table 2
[36–44].

CDs assist CuNCs@3-MPA in constructing Z-type heterostructures for directed generation of ·O2 − and improve the photocatalytic efficiency of MO. The abundant Cu(I) content of CuNCs@3-MPA/CDs involves in the Fenton-like reaction which makes them possess ultra-high peroxidase-like activity. This method organically combines two energy-level controllable materials to catalyze substrate reactions by directed radical generation, which greatly expands the utilization and application of CuNCs and CDs, with important research significance.

Dan Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xiangshan Lei: Writing – review & editing. Qingdong Liu: Writing – review & editing. Yajun Chen: Writing – review & editing. Jiao Wang: Writing – review & editing. Bingyan Han: Conceptualization, Resources, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Gaohong He: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Liaoning Key Laboratory of Chemical Additive Synthesis and Separation (ZJKF2103 & ZJKF2011) and Science Fund for Creative Research Groups of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22021005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2023.124133.

ce:section.ce:para:

Li, Superoxide Radical-Mediated Self-Synthesized Au/MoO3-x Hybrids with Enhanced Peroxidase-like Activity and Photothermal Effect for Anti-MRSA Therapy, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 14 (2022) 13025-13037. doi: 10.1021/acsami.1c23676.
